findings.description: Bare `except:` clause silently swallows ALL exceptions including KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit, MemoryError, and programming errors (AttributeError, TypeError, etc.). No error information is logged or surfaced.
findings.evidence: `except: pass` with no exception type, no logging of the caught exception, no re-raise. Retries proceed regardless of exception type, including non-transient or fatal errors.

findings.description: No logging of the actual exception inside the retry loop. The caller and operator have no visibility into why fetches are failing — network error, DNS failure, parse error, and programming bugs are all indistinguishable.
findings.evidence: The `except` block is `pass` — no `logger.exception`, no `logger.warning`, no captured traceback. The only downstream signal is a warning after all 3 attempts are exhausted (line 35).

findings.description: KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit are caught and suppressed by the bare `except`. A user attempting to abort the process mid-retry will be silently ignored for up to 3 loop iterations.
findings.evidence: `except:` in Python catches BaseException subclasses including KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit. The loop will `pass` on these and proceed to the next attempt.

findings.description: Retry logic does not distinguish transient errors (network timeout) from permanent errors (malformed URL, permission denied, server 404). All 3 attempts will be exhausted even when retrying is pointless.
findings.evidence: The bare `except` with unconditional retry treats all exceptions identically. A `ValueError` from a bad URL will be retried 3 times unnecessarily.

findings.description: No exponential backoff or delay between retry attempts. If the upstream is rate-limiting or briefly overloaded, 3 immediate retries may worsen the situation or trigger rate-limit bans.
findings.evidence: Loop `for attempt in range(3)` with no `time.sleep` between iterations.

findings.description: `process_feed` does not handle the case where `output_dir` does not exist. `output_path.write_text(...)` will raise `FileNotFoundError`, which propagates uncaught to the caller.
findings.evidence: `Path(output_dir) / 'latest_feed.json'` followed by `.write_text(...)` with no `mkdir` or existence check.

findings.description: `process_feed` overwrites `latest_feed.json` in place with no atomic write or backup. A crash mid-write (e.g. disk full) corrupts the file, and the previous good data is lost.

When `rows` is truthy but all rows fail the `id`/`timestamp` filter, `valid_rows` is empty and an empty JSON array `[]` is written. The function still returns `True`, misleading the caller into thinking valid data was stored.
findings.evidence: `valid_rows = [r for r in rows if r.get('id') and r.get('timestamp')]` — if no rows pass, `valid_rows` is `[]`; `output_path.write_text(json.dumps([], indent=2))` runs; `return True` is reached.

findings.description: No test or monitor for the retry loop behavior, the silent-swallow of exceptions, or the empty-valid-rows edge case.
findings.evidence: No test file present in the artifact; no assertion, no mock of `urlopen`, no coverage of the bare-except path.

clean.location: lines 46 (id/timestamp filter logic — filter itself is correct)
